function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs
users_2016 = set()
for user in users_2016_raw:
    users_2016.add(user['user_id'])

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Method 1: Look for pattern "services in X, Y, and Z."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', services_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 2: Look for pattern "including X, Y, and Z."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            including_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', including_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 3: Look for pattern "offers X, Y, and Z services"
    if not categories and 'offers' in description and 'services' in description:
        match = re.search(r'offers (.+?) services', description)
        if match:
            offers_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', offers_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and', 'a range of']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Fallback: Extract anything that looks like a category (capitalized phrases)
    if not categories:
        # Find sequences of words with capital letters
        matches = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)\b', description)
        # Filter out common words and location words
        exclude_words = {'Located', 'at', 'this', 'facility', 'offers', 'and', 'or', 'in', 'the', 'a', 'an', 'for', 'with', 'range', 'of', 'services'}
        categories = [m for m in matches if m not in exclude_words and len(m) > 3]
    
    # Store the categories
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Uncategorized']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    user_id = review['user_id']
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    if user_id in users_2016:
        # Convert business_ref (businessref_X) to business_id (businessid_X)
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        # Get categories for this business
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Uncategorized'])
        
        # Count one review for each category (Yelp-style, businesses can have multiple categories)
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = sorted(category_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format result
result = {
    'top_5_categories': [
        {'category': cat, 'review_count': count} 
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ],
    'total_2016_users': len(users_2016),
    'total_reviews_analyzed': sum(category_review_counts.values())
}

# Print in required format
print("----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----")
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))
print("----END PRINT FORMAT----")"}

    categories = []
    
    # Extract categories from description text
    # Pattern 1: "services in Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 2: "including Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$|\s+for|\s+making)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 3: Look for capitalized phrases
    if not categories:
        phrases = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z&\s]*)\b', description)
        # Filter out non-category words
        exclude = {'Located', 'at', 'in', 'and', 'or', 'this', 'that', 'facility', 'establishment', 'offers', 'services', 'range', 'including', 'for', 'your', 'making', 'making it'}
        categories = [p.strip() for p in phrases if p.strip() and len(p.strip()) > 3 and p.strip() not in exclude]
        # Take only the last few which are likely categories
        if len(categories) > 1:
            categories = categories[-4:]  # Last 4 are usually the categories
    
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Other']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    if review['user_id'] in users_2016:
        business_ref = review['business_ref']
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Other'])
        for category in categories:
            category_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5
top_5 = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format final answer
result = "Top 5 business categories by total reviews from 2016-registered users:\n\n"
for i, (category, count) in enumerate(top_5, 1):
    result += f"{i}. {category}: {count} reviews\n"

print(result)"}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Among users who registered on Yelp in 2016, which 5 business categories have received the most total reviews from those users since 2016?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. businessinfo_database
   - This database is stored in a MongoDB database and contains metadata and user check-in records for Yelp businesses. It captures business attributes, service descriptions, operating hours, and user visit timestamps.
   - This database consists of two collections:
     - business
       - This collection contains metadata for businesses listed on Yelp, including names, identifiers, operational status, attributes, business hours, and service/location descriptions.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Unique business identifier
         - name (str): Business name
         - review_count (int): Total number of reviews
         - is_open (int): Whether business is currently open (1=open, 0=closed)
         - attributes (dict or null): Business attributes (parking, WiFi, etc.)
         - hours (dict or null): Operating hours
         - description (str): Business description including location information

     - checkin
       - This collection records the check-in behavior of Yelp users at various businesses, capturing the timestamped log of user presence.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Business identifier linking to business collection
         - date (list of str): List of check-in timestamps

2. user_database
   - This database is stored in a DuckDB database and contains user profiles and user-generated content from Yelp, including written reviews, tips, and engagement statistics such as review counts, votes, and elite status.
   - This database consists of three tables:
     - review
       - This table contains user-generated reviews of businesses on Yelp, including rating scores, review content, and metadata such as user and business reference identifiers.
       - Fields:
         - review_id (str): Unique review identifier
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - rating (int): Rating score (1-5)
         - useful (int): Useful votes received
         - funny (int): Funny votes received
         - cool (int): Cool votes received
         - text (str): Review text content
         - date (str): Review date

     - tip
       - This table contains short user tips about Yelp businesses, usually concise suggestions or comments, along with optional compliments and timestamps.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - text (str): Tip text content
         - date (str): Tip date
         - compliment_count (int): Number of compliments received

     - user
       - This table contains user profile information from Yelp, including activity statistics and membership history.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str): Unique user identifier
         - name (str): User name
         - review_count (int): Total reviews written by user
         - yelping_since (str): Registration date
         - useful (int): Total useful votes received
         - funny (int): Total funny votes received
         - cool (int): Total cool votes received
         - elite (str): Elite status years

HINTS:
- The "business_id" field in the business collection corresponds to the "business_ref" fields in both the review table and the tip table. The values differ only by their prefixes: "business_id" uses the prefix `businessid_`, while "business_ref" uses the prefix `businessref_`. For example, `businessid_1` in the business collection corresponds to `businessref_1` in the review and tip tables.
- The datasets contain five tables/collections in total. Carefully identify which tables or collections and which fields contain the information required to answer the query.
- The business collection's "description" field includes location information if needed.
- The business collection's "attributes" field includes services information if needed.

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get reviews by 2016-registered users
reviews_result_key = 'var_functions.query_db:16'
reviews_raw = locals().get(reviews_result_key, [])

# Format reviews data for easier processing
reviews = []
for review in reviews_raw:
    reviews.append({
        'user_id': review['user_id'],
        'business_ref': review['business_ref'],
        'date': review['date']
    })

# Get business_id to business_ref mappings
business_mappings = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # business_ref looks like "businessref_74", business_id looks like "businessid_74"
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[1]
    business_mappings[business_id] = business_ref

# Get all businesses from MongoDB to extract categories
all_businesses_key = 'var_functions.query_db:14'
businesses_sample = locals().get(all_businesses_key, [])

# Build a dictionary of business_id to category list
category_cache = {}

# Sample parsing logic (from the example)
for biz in businesses_sample:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    # Parse the description to extract categories
    # Pattern: "services in X, Y, Z, and W" or "including X, Y, and Z"
    categories = []
    
    # "services in" pattern
    if 'services in' in description:
        # Find the part after "services in"
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+)$', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Clean up and split
            services_text = services_text.replace('services including', '')
            # Split by commas and clean up each category
            if ',' in services_text:
                # Handle comma-separated list
                parts = [p.strip() for p in services_text.split(',')]
                
                # Handle "and" before last item
                if len(parts) > 1 and 'and ' in parts[-1]:
                    # Split the last item by "and"
                    last_parts = parts[-1].split('and')
                    parts = parts[:-1] + [p.strip() for p in last_parts]
                
                # Filter out empty strings and common phrases
                for part in parts:
                    part = part.strip()
                    if part and part != 'Food' and part not in ['Located at', 'this', 'establishment offers']:
                        # Clean up further
                        part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)  # Remove trailing period
                        part = re.sub(r'\bincluding\b', '', part).strip()
                        if part and len(part) > 2:  # Avoid too short strings
                            categories.append(part)
            else:
                # No commas, just one category
                services_text = re.sub(r'\.$', '', services_text)
                if services_text:
                    categories.append(services_text)
    
    # "including" pattern (alternative)
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match1 = re.search(r'including (.+)$', description)
        if match1:
            including_text = match1.group(1)
            # Split by commas and and
            parts = []
            if ', ' in including_text:
                # Multiple items separated by commas
                parts = [p.strip() for p in including_text.split(',')]
                # Split last part if it contains "and"
                if len(parts) > 0 and ' and ' in parts[-1]:
                    parts = parts[:-1] + [p.strip() for p in parts[-1].split('and')]
            elif ' and ' in including_text:
                # Multiple items separated by "and"
                parts = [p.strip() for p in including_text.split(' and ')]
            else:
                # Single item
                parts = [including_text]
            
            # Clean up
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and len(part) > 2:
                    part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # If no parsing worked, try a simple comma split
    if not categories:
        # Look for comma-separated items
        simple_split = description.split(',')
        if len(simple_split) > 2:
            for part in simple_split:
                part = part.strip()
                # Skip location info
                if 'Located at' in part:
                    continue
                # Clean up
                part = re.sub(r'^(this|establishment|facility|business offers?|and)\b', '', part, flags=re.IGNORECASE).strip()
                part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)
                if part and len(part) > 2 and part not in ['the', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Store in cache
    if categories:
        category_cache[business_id] = categories
    else:
        # Fallback: use the whole description or key parts
        category_cache[business_id] = [description] if description else []

# Print the cache for debugging
print("Category cache (sample):")
for k, v in list(category_cache.items())[:5]:
    print(f"{k}: {v}")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Step 1: Get all 2016-registered users
users_2016_key = 'var_functions.query_db:12'
users_2016_raw = locals().get(users_2016_key, [])

users_2016 = set()
for user in users_2016_raw:
    users_2016.add(user['user_id'])

# Step 2: Get all reviews by these users since 2016
reviews_key = 'var_functions.query_db:16'
reviews_raw = locals().get(reviews_key, [])

# Filter reviews to only those by 2016 users and get unique business refs
business_refs_to_check = set()
user_business_reviews = defaultdict(int)  # (user_id, business_ref) -> count

for review in reviews_raw:
    user_id = review['user_id']
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    if user_id in users_2016:
        business_refs_to_check.add(business_ref)
        user_business_reviews[(user_id, business_ref)] += 1

# Step 3: Get all business information from MongoDB to parse categories
# First, let's check how many businesses we have
all_businesses_key = 'var_functions.query_db:14'
businesses_sample = locals().get(all_businesses_key, [])

# We need to get ALL businesses to build a complete mapping
result_str = json.dumps({
    'num_2016_users': len(users_2016),
    'num_business_refs_needed': len(business_refs_to_check),
    'sample_businesses': len(businesses_sample)
})

print("----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----")
print("__RESULT__:")
print(result_str)
print("----END PRINT FORMAT----")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs
users_2016 = set()
for user in users_2016_raw:
    users_2016.add(user['user_id'])

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Method 1: Look for pattern "services in X, Y, and Z."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', services_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 2: Look for pattern "including X, Y, and Z."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            including_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', including_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 3: Look for pattern "offers X, Y, and Z services"
    if not categories and 'offers' in description and 'services' in description:
        match = re.search(r'offers (.+?) services', description)
        if match:
            offers_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', offers_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and', 'a range of']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Fallback: Extract anything that looks like a category (capitalized phrases)
    if not categories:
        # Find sequences of words with capital letters
        matches = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)\b', description)
        # Filter out common words and location words
        exclude_words = {'Located', 'at', 'this', 'facility', 'offers', 'and', 'or', 'in', 'the', 'a', 'an', 'for', 'with', 'range', 'of', 'services'}
        categories = [m for m in matches if m not in exclude_words and len(m) > 3]
    
    # Store the categories
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Uncategorized']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    user_id = review['user_id']
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    if user_id in users_2016:
        # Convert business_ref (businessref_X) to business_id (businessid_X)
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        # Get categories for this business
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Uncategorized'])
        
        # Count one review for each category (Yelp-style, businesses can have multiple categories)
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = sorted(category_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format result
result = {
    'top_5_categories': [
        {'category': cat, 'review_count': count} 
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ],
    'total_2016_users': len(users_2016),
    'total_reviews_analyzed': sum(category_review_counts.values())
}

# Print in required format
print("----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----")
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))
print("----END PRINT FORMAT----")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs
users_2016 = set([user['user_id'] for user in users_2016_raw])

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Extract categories from description text
    # Pattern 1: "services in Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 2: "including Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$|\s+for|\s+making)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 3: Look for capitalized phrases
    if not categories:
        phrases = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z&\s]*)\b', description)
        # Filter out non-category words
        exclude = {'Located', 'at', 'in', 'and', 'or', 'this', 'that', 'facility', 'establishment', 'offers', 'services', 'range', 'including', 'for', 'your', 'making', 'making it'}
        categories = [p.strip() for p in phrases if p.strip() and len(p.strip()) > 3 and p.strip() not in exclude]
        # Take only the last few which are likely categories
        if len(categories) > 1:
            categories = categories[-4:]  # Last 4 are usually the categories
    
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Other']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    if review['user_id'] in users_2016:
        business_ref = review['business_ref']
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Other'])
        for category in categories:
            category_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5
top_5 = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format final answer
result = "Top 5 business categories by total reviews from 2016-registered users:\n\n"
for i, (category, count) in enumerate(top_5, 1):
    result += f"{i}. {category}: {count} reviews\n"

print(result)"}

messages.content:
You are a data analysis agent. Use only the tools listed below to answer the user's query, based on the provided DATABASE DESCRIPTION for logical database names and their types (SQL or MongoDB), and the results of previous tool calls.

TOOLS (system will execute):
- query_db: run a SQL or Mongo query. Returns a list of JSON-serializable records or an error string.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>", "query": "<SQL or Mongo query>"}
- list_db: list tables or collections for a given database.
  Required args: {"db_name": "<logical_db_name>"}
- execute_python: run Python code.
  Required args: {"code": "<python_code>"}
- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.
2. Inside execute_python code you may read storage entries using the provided key names, e.g., if the tool call id is 'functions.tool:id', you can access its result via `locals()['var_functions.tool:id']` in your code.
3. You cannot modify or reassign those storage-provided variables; you may read them and create new variables as needed.
4. If a tool result is large, the next message will include a preview (first 10000 characters) and the storage entry will be the .json file path (a string) where the full result is stored. To access the full result, your execute_python code must open and read that .json file.

KEY RULES (must follow exactly):
1. Always use tool calls. Do not output plain text, explanations, or reasoning.
2. Include all required arguments for the tool you call.
3. For query_db, always specify db_name and query. Refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION for db_name and query format.
4. For PostgreSQL, wrap mixed-case or uppercase column names in double quotes.
5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.
6. Use execute_python for data processing as needed. 
7. When using execute_python, your code will be quoted by triple double-quotes and passed as a string to `exec(...)` for execution in a Python 3.12 environment with only pandas and pyarrow installed. So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. Do not use non-built-in or non-installed packages.
8. When using execute_python, your code must print the result at the end exactly as shown in the PRINT FORMAT section below. The printed result must be a string that can be successfully parsed by json.loads() without errors.

PRINT FORMAT (must match exactly):
----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----
print("__RESULT__:")
print(your_json_serializable_string_here)
----END PRINT FORMAT----

For simple types (int, float, str, bool, None), you may use json.dumps() to produce a valid JSON string.
For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Among users who registered on Yelp in 2016, which 5 business categories have received the most total reviews from those users since 2016?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. businessinfo_database
   - This database is stored in a MongoDB database and contains metadata and user check-in records for Yelp businesses. It captures business attributes, service descriptions, operating hours, and user visit timestamps.
   - This database consists of two collections:
     - business
       - This collection contains metadata for businesses listed on Yelp, including names, identifiers, operational status, attributes, business hours, and service/location descriptions.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Unique business identifier
         - name (str): Business name
         - review_count (int): Total number of reviews
         - is_open (int): Whether business is currently open (1=open, 0=closed)
         - attributes (dict or null): Business attributes (parking, WiFi, etc.)
         - hours (dict or null): Operating hours
         - description (str): Business description including location information

     - checkin
       - This collection records the check-in behavior of Yelp users at various businesses, capturing the timestamped log of user presence.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Business identifier linking to business collection
         - date (list of str): List of check-in timestamps

2. user_database
   - This database is stored in a DuckDB database and contains user profiles and user-generated content from Yelp, including written reviews, tips, and engagement statistics such as review counts, votes, and elite status.
   - This database consists of three tables:
     - review
       - This table contains user-generated reviews of businesses on Yelp, including rating scores, review content, and metadata such as user and business reference identifiers.
       - Fields:
         - review_id (str): Unique review identifier
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - rating (int): Rating score (1-5)
         - useful (int): Useful votes received
         - funny (int): Funny votes received
         - cool (int): Cool votes received
         - text (str): Review text content
         - date (str): Review date

     - tip
       - This table contains short user tips about Yelp businesses, usually concise suggestions or comments, along with optional compliments and timestamps.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - text (str): Tip text content
         - date (str): Tip date
         - compliment_count (int): Number of compliments received

     - user
       - This table contains user profile information from Yelp, including activity statistics and membership history.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str): Unique user identifier
         - name (str): User name
         - review_count (int): Total reviews written by user
         - yelping_since (str): Registration date
         - useful (int): Total useful votes received
         - funny (int): Total funny votes received
         - cool (int): Total cool votes received
         - elite (str): Elite status years

HINTS:
- The "business_id" field in the business collection corresponds to the "business_ref" fields in both the review table and the tip table. The values differ only by their prefixes: "business_id" uses the prefix `businessid_`, while "business_ref" uses the prefix `businessref_`. For example, `businessid_1` in the business collection corresponds to `businessref_1` in the review and tip tables.
- The datasets contain five tables/collections in total. Carefully identify which tables or collections and which fields contain the information required to answer the query.
- The business collection's "description" field includes location information if needed.
- The business collection's "attributes" field includes services information if needed.

# Format reviews data for easier processing
reviews = []
for review in reviews_raw:
    reviews.append({
        'user_id': review['user_id'],
        'business_ref': review['business_ref'],
        'date': review['date']
    })

# Get business_id to business_ref mappings
business_mappings = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # business_ref looks like "businessref_74", business_id looks like "businessid_74"
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[1]
    business_mappings[business_id] = business_ref

# Get all businesses from MongoDB to extract categories
all_businesses_key = 'var_functions.query_db:14'
businesses_sample = locals().get(all_businesses_key, [])

# Build a dictionary of business_id to category list
category_cache = {}

# Sample parsing logic (from the example)
for biz in businesses_sample:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    # Parse the description to extract categories
    # Pattern: "services in X, Y, Z, and W" or "including X, Y, and Z"
    categories = []
    
    # "services in" pattern
    if 'services in' in description:
        # Find the part after "services in"
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+)$', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Clean up and split
            services_text = services_text.replace('services including', '')
            # Split by commas and clean up each category
            if ',' in services_text:
                # Handle comma-separated list
                parts = [p.strip() for p in services_text.split(',')]
                
                # Handle "and" before last item
                if len(parts) > 1 and 'and ' in parts[-1]:
                    # Split the last item by "and"
                    last_parts = parts[-1].split('and')
                    parts = parts[:-1] + [p.strip() for p in last_parts]
                
                # Filter out empty strings and common phrases
                for part in parts:
                    part = part.strip()
                    if part and part != 'Food' and part not in ['Located at', 'this', 'establishment offers']:
                        # Clean up further
                        part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)  # Remove trailing period
                        part = re.sub(r'\bincluding\b', '', part).strip()
                        if part and len(part) > 2:  # Avoid too short strings
                            categories.append(part)
            else:
                # No commas, just one category
                services_text = re.sub(r'\.$', '', services_text)
                if services_text:
                    categories.append(services_text)
    
    # "including" pattern (alternative)
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match1 = re.search(r'including (.+)$', description)
        if match1:
            including_text = match1.group(1)
            # Split by commas and and
            parts = []
            if ', ' in including_text:
                # Multiple items separated by commas
                parts = [p.strip() for p in including_text.split(',')]
                # Split last part if it contains "and"
                if len(parts) > 0 and ' and ' in parts[-1]:
                    parts = parts[:-1] + [p.strip() for p in parts[-1].split('and')]
            elif ' and ' in including_text:
                # Multiple items separated by "and"
                parts = [p.strip() for p in including_text.split(' and ')]
            else:
                # Single item
                parts = [including_text]
            
            # Clean up
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and len(part) > 2:
                    part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # If no parsing worked, try a simple comma split
    if not categories:
        # Look for comma-separated items
        simple_split = description.split(',')
        if len(simple_split) > 2:
            for part in simple_split:
                part = part.strip()
                # Skip location info
                if 'Located at' in part:
                    continue
                # Clean up
                part = re.sub(r'^(this|establishment|facility|business offers?|and)\b', '', part, flags=re.IGNORECASE).strip()
                part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)
                if part and len(part) > 2 and part not in ['the', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Store in cache
    if categories:
        category_cache[business_id] = categories
    else:
        # Fallback: use the whole description or key parts
        category_cache[business_id] = [description] if description else []

# Print the cache for debugging
print("Category cache (sample):")
for k, v in list(category_cache.items())[:5]:
    print(f"{k}: {v}")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs
users_2016 = set()
for user in users_2016_raw:
    users_2016.add(user['user_id'])

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Method 1: Look for pattern "services in X, Y, and Z."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', services_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 2: Look for pattern "including X, Y, and Z."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            including_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', including_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 3: Look for pattern "offers X, Y, and Z services"
    if not categories and 'offers' in description and 'services' in description:
        match = re.search(r'offers (.+?) services', description)
        if match:
            offers_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', offers_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and', 'a range of']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Fallback: Extract anything that looks like a category (capitalized phrases)
    if not categories:
        # Find sequences of words with capital letters
        matches = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)\b', description)
        # Filter out common words and location words
        exclude_words = {'Located', 'at', 'this', 'facility', 'offers', 'and', 'or', 'in', 'the', 'a', 'an', 'for', 'with', 'range', 'of', 'services'}
        categories = [m for m in matches if m not in exclude_words and len(m) > 3]
    
    # Store the categories
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Uncategorized']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    user_id = review['user_id']
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    if user_id in users_2016:
        # Convert business_ref (businessref_X) to business_id (businessid_X)
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        # Get categories for this business
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Uncategorized'])
        
        # Count one review for each category (Yelp-style, businesses can have multiple categories)
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = sorted(category_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format result
result = {
    'top_5_categories': [
        {'category': cat, 'review_count': count} 
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ],
    'total_2016_users': len(users_2016),
    'total_reviews_analyzed': sum(category_review_counts.values())
}

# Print in required format
print("----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----")
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))
print("----END PRINT FORMAT----")"}

{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs
users_2016 = set([user['user_id'] for user in users_2016_raw])

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Extract categories from description text
    # Pattern 1: "services in Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 2: "including Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$|\s+for|\s+making)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 3: Look for capitalized phrases
    if not categories:
        phrases = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z&\s]*)\b', description)
        # Filter out non-category words
        exclude = {'Located', 'at', 'in', 'and', 'or', 'this', 'that', 'facility', 'establishment', 'offers', 'services', 'range', 'including', 'for', 'your', 'making', 'making it'}
        categories = [p.strip() for p in phrases if p.strip() and len(p.strip()) > 3 and p.strip() not in exclude]
        # Take only the last few which are likely categories
        if len(categories) > 1:
            categories = categories[-4:]  # Last 4 are usually the categories
    
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Other']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    if review['user_id'] in users_2016:
        business_ref = review['business_ref']
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Other'])
        for category in categories:
            category_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5
top_5 = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format final answer
result = "Top 5 business categories by total reviews from 2016-registered users:\n\n"
for i, (category, count) in enumerate(top_5, 1):
    result += f"{i}. {category}: {count} reviews\n"

print(result)"}

TOOLS (system will execute):

- execute_python: run Python code.

- return_answer: finish and return the final answer (plain text).
  Required args: {"answer": "<final plain-text answer>"}

INSTRUCTIONS:
1. After each tool call, its result will be stored in a storage under a key named after the tool call id (you will be told the key name). The next message will include both the result (or a preview if it's large) and the storage key name.

5. For list_db, refer the DATABASE DESCRIPTION to specify db_name.

So you must ensure your code is compatible with this execution method. For exampe, do not use triple double-quotes in your code, as they may interfere with parsing. For complex or non-JSON-serializable types, you must convert them into JSON-compatible forms before printing.
For lists or dictionaries, you must ensure that all nested elements are also converted into JSON-serializable types.
9. Return the final answer only via a single return_answer tool call. Do not include extra text, explanation, or formatting.

EXAMPLES:
- query_db:
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "SELECT * FROM some_table LIMIT some_limit;"}} # for SQL databases
{"tool": "query_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name", "query": "{"collection": "some_collection", "filter": {some_filter}, "projection": {some_projection}, "limit": some_limit}"}} # for MongoDB databases

- list_db:
{"tool": "list_db", "args": {"db_name": "some_db_name"}}

- execute_python:
{"tool": "execute_python", "args": {"code": "import pandas as pd
# rl1 and rl2 are the keys of two JSON-serializable record lists in storage
df1 = pd.DataFrame(rl1)
df2 = pd.DataFrame(rl2)
result = pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id').head(10).to_json(orient='records')
print('__RESULT__:')
print(result)"}}

- return_answer:
{"tool": "return_answer", "args": {"answer": "…final plain-text answer…"}}

If you cannot proceed, call return_answer with a short explanatory message.

Do not output explanations, reasoning, or any natural language outside of the required tool calls.

QUERY:
Among users who registered on Yelp in 2016, which 5 business categories have received the most total reviews from those users since 2016?

DATABASE DESCRIPTION:
You are working with two databases to solve this query.

Here are the descriptions of these two databases:

1. businessinfo_database
   - This database is stored in a MongoDB database and contains metadata and user check-in records for Yelp businesses. It captures business attributes, service descriptions, operating hours, and user visit timestamps.
   - This database consists of two collections:
     - business
       - This collection contains metadata for businesses listed on Yelp, including names, identifiers, operational status, attributes, business hours, and service/location descriptions.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Unique business identifier
         - name (str): Business name
         - review_count (int): Total number of reviews
         - is_open (int): Whether business is currently open (1=open, 0=closed)
         - attributes (dict or null): Business attributes (parking, WiFi, etc.)
         - hours (dict or null): Operating hours
         - description (str): Business description including location information

     - checkin
       - This collection records the check-in behavior of Yelp users at various businesses, capturing the timestamped log of user presence.
       - Fields:
         - _id
         - business_id (str): Business identifier linking to business collection
         - date (list of str): List of check-in timestamps

2. user_database
   - This database is stored in a DuckDB database and contains user profiles and user-generated content from Yelp, including written reviews, tips, and engagement statistics such as review counts, votes, and elite status.
   - This database consists of three tables:
     - review
       - This table contains user-generated reviews of businesses on Yelp, including rating scores, review content, and metadata such as user and business reference identifiers.
       - Fields:
         - review_id (str): Unique review identifier
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - rating (int): Rating score (1-5)
         - useful (int): Useful votes received
         - funny (int): Funny votes received
         - cool (int): Cool votes received
         - text (str): Review text content
         - date (str): Review date

     - tip
       - This table contains short user tips about Yelp businesses, usually concise suggestions or comments, along with optional compliments and timestamps.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str or null): User identifier
         - business_ref (str): Business identifier linking to the business collection
         - text (str): Tip text content
         - date (str): Tip date
         - compliment_count (int): Number of compliments received

     - user
       - This table contains user profile information from Yelp, including activity statistics and membership history.
       - Fields:
         - user_id (str): Unique user identifier
         - name (str): User name
         - review_count (int): Total reviews written by user
         - yelping_since (str): Registration date
         - useful (int): Total useful votes received
         - funny (int): Total funny votes received
         - cool (int): Total cool votes received
         - elite (str): Elite status years

HINTS:
- The "business_id" field in the business collection corresponds to the "business_ref" fields in both the review table and the tip table. The values differ only by their prefixes: "business_id" uses the prefix `businessid_`, while "business_ref" uses the prefix `businessref_`. For example, `businessid_1` in the business collection corresponds to `businessref_1` in the review and tip tables.
- The datasets contain five tables/collections in total. Carefully identify which tables or collections and which fields contain the information required to answer the query.
- The business collection's "description" field includes location information if needed.
- The business collection's "attributes" field includes services information if needed.

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get reviews by 2016-registered users
reviews_result_key = 'var_functions.query_db:16'
reviews_raw = locals().get(reviews_result_key, [])

# Format reviews data for easier processing
reviews = []
for review in reviews_raw:
    reviews.append({
        'user_id': review['user_id'],
        'business_ref': review['business_ref'],
        'date': review['date']
    })

# Get business_id to business_ref mappings
business_mappings = {}
for review in reviews:
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    # business_ref looks like "businessref_74", business_id looks like "businessid_74"
    business_id = 'businessid_' + business_ref.split('_')[1]
    business_mappings[business_id] = business_ref

# Get all businesses from MongoDB to extract categories
all_businesses_key = 'var_functions.query_db:14'
businesses_sample = locals().get(all_businesses_key, [])

# Build a dictionary of business_id to category list
category_cache = {}

# Sample parsing logic (from the example)
for biz in businesses_sample:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    # Parse the description to extract categories
    # Pattern: "services in X, Y, Z, and W" or "including X, Y, and Z"
    categories = []
    
    # "services in" pattern
    if 'services in' in description:
        # Find the part after "services in"
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+)$', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Clean up and split
            services_text = services_text.replace('services including', '')
            # Split by commas and clean up each category
            if ',' in services_text:
                # Handle comma-separated list
                parts = [p.strip() for p in services_text.split(',')]
                
                # Handle "and" before last item
                if len(parts) > 1 and 'and ' in parts[-1]:
                    # Split the last item by "and"
                    last_parts = parts[-1].split('and')
                    parts = parts[:-1] + [p.strip() for p in last_parts]
                
                # Filter out empty strings and common phrases
                for part in parts:
                    part = part.strip()
                    if part and part != 'Food' and part not in ['Located at', 'this', 'establishment offers']:
                        # Clean up further
                        part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)  # Remove trailing period
                        part = re.sub(r'\bincluding\b', '', part).strip()
                        if part and len(part) > 2:  # Avoid too short strings
                            categories.append(part)
            else:
                # No commas, just one category
                services_text = re.sub(r'\.$', '', services_text)
                if services_text:
                    categories.append(services_text)
    
    # "including" pattern (alternative)
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match1 = re.search(r'including (.+)$', description)
        if match1:
            including_text = match1.group(1)
            # Split by commas and and
            parts = []
            if ', ' in including_text:
                # Multiple items separated by commas
                parts = [p.strip() for p in including_text.split(',')]
                # Split last part if it contains "and"
                if len(parts) > 0 and ' and ' in parts[-1]:
                    parts = parts[:-1] + [p.strip() for p in parts[-1].split('and')]
            elif ' and ' in including_text:
                # Multiple items separated by "and"
                parts = [p.strip() for p in including_text.split(' and ')]
            else:
                # Single item
                parts = [including_text]
            
            # Clean up
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and len(part) > 2:
                    part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # If no parsing worked, try a simple comma split
    if not categories:
        # Look for comma-separated items
        simple_split = description.split(',')
        if len(simple_split) > 2:
            for part in simple_split:
                part = part.strip()
                # Skip location info
                if 'Located at' in part:
                    continue
                # Clean up
                part = re.sub(r'^(this|establishment|facility|business offers?|and)\b', '', part, flags=re.IGNORECASE).strip()
                part = re.sub(r'\.$', '', part)
                if part and len(part) > 2 and part not in ['the', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Store in cache
    if categories:
        category_cache[business_id] = categories
    else:
        # Fallback: use the whole description or key parts
        category_cache[business_id] = [description] if description else []

# Print the cache for debugging
print("Category cache (sample):")
for k, v in list(category_cache.items())[:5]:
    print(f"{k}: {v}")"}

import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Step 1: Get all 2016-registered users
users_2016_key = 'var_functions.query_db:12'
users_2016_raw = locals().get(users_2016_key, [])

users_2016 = set()
for user in users_2016_raw:
    users_2016.add(user['user_id'])

# Step 2: Get all reviews by these users since 2016
reviews_key = 'var_functions.query_db:16'
reviews_raw = locals().get(reviews_key, [])

# Filter reviews to only those by 2016 users and get unique business refs
business_refs_to_check = set()
user_business_reviews = defaultdict(int)  # (user_id, business_ref) -> count

for review in reviews_raw:
    user_id = review['user_id']
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    if user_id in users_2016:
        business_refs_to_check.add(business_ref)
        user_business_reviews[(user_id, business_ref)] += 1

# Step 3: Get all business information from MongoDB to parse categories
# First, let's check how many businesses we have
all_businesses_key = 'var_functions.query_db:14'
businesses_sample = locals().get(all_businesses_key, [])

# We need to get ALL businesses to build a complete mapping
result_str = json.dumps({
    'num_2016_users': len(users_2016),
    'num_business_refs_needed': len(business_refs_to_check),
    'sample_businesses': len(businesses_sample)
})

print("----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----")
print("__RESULT__:")
print(result_str)
print("----END PRINT FORMAT----")"}

function.arguments:
{"code": "import json
import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs
users_2016 = set()
for user in users_2016_raw:
    users_2016.add(user['user_id'])

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Method 1: Look for pattern "services in X, Y, and Z."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            services_text = match.group(1)
            # Split by comma and 'and'
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', services_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 2: Look for pattern "including X, Y, and Z."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            including_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', including_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Method 3: Look for pattern "offers X, Y, and Z services"
    if not categories and 'offers' in description and 'services' in description:
        match = re.search(r'offers (.+?) services', description)
        if match:
            offers_text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', offers_text)
            for part in parts:
                part = part.strip()
                if part and part not in ['', 'and', 'a range of']:
                    categories.append(part)
    
    # Fallback: Extract anything that looks like a category (capitalized phrases)
    if not categories:
        # Find sequences of words with capital letters
        matches = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-z]+(?:\s+[A-Z][a-z]+)*)\b', description)
        # Filter out common words and location words
        exclude_words = {'Located', 'at', 'this', 'facility', 'offers', 'and', 'or', 'in', 'the', 'a', 'an', 'for', 'with', 'range', 'of', 'services'}
        categories = [m for m in matches if m not in exclude_words and len(m) > 3]
    
    # Store the categories
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Uncategorized']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_review_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    user_id = review['user_id']
    business_ref = review['business_ref']
    
    if user_id in users_2016:
        # Convert business_ref (businessref_X) to business_id (businessid_X)
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        # Get categories for this business
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Uncategorized'])
        
        # Count one review for each category (Yelp-style, businesses can have multiple categories)
        for category in categories:
            category_review_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5 categories
top_5_categories = sorted(category_review_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format result
result = {
    'top_5_categories': [
        {'category': cat, 'review_count': count} 
        for cat, count in top_5_categories
    ],
    'total_2016_users': len(users_2016),
    'total_reviews_analyzed': sum(category_review_counts.values())
}

# Print in required format
print("----BEGIN PRINT FORMAT----")
print("__RESULT__:")
print(json.dumps(result))
print("----END PRINT FORMAT----")"}

import re
from collections import defaultdict

# Get all data from previous queries
users_2016_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:12', [])
reviews_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:16', [])
businesses_raw = locals().get('var_functions.query_db:22', [])

# Step 1: Create set of 2016 user IDs

# Step 2: Build mapping from business_id to categories
# Parse description field to extract categories
business_categories = {}

for biz in businesses_raw:
    business_id = biz['business_id']
    description = biz.get('description', '')
    
    categories = []
    
    # Extract categories from description text
    # Pattern 1: "services in Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if 'services in' in description:
        match = re.search(r'services in (.+?)(?:\.|$)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 2: "including Category1, Category2, and Category3."
    if not categories and 'including' in description:
        match = re.search(r'including (.+?)(?:\.|$|\s+for|\s+making)', description)
        if match:
            text = match.group(1)
            parts = re.split(r',\s*|\sand\s', text)
            categories = [p.strip() for p in parts if p.strip() and p.strip() != 'and']
    
    # Pattern 3: Look for capitalized phrases
    if not categories:
        phrases = re.findall(r'\b([A-Z][a-zA-Z&\s]*)\b', description)
        # Filter out non-category words
        exclude = {'Located', 'at', 'in', 'and', 'or', 'this', 'that', 'facility', 'establishment', 'offers', 'services', 'range', 'including', 'for', 'your', 'making', 'making it'}
        categories = [p.strip() for p in phrases if p.strip() and len(p.strip()) > 3 and p.strip() not in exclude]
        # Take only the last few which are likely categories
        if len(categories) > 1:
            categories = categories[-4:]  # Last 4 are usually the categories
    
    business_categories[business_id] = categories if categories else ['Other']

# Step 3: Count reviews per category
category_counts = defaultdict(int)

for review in reviews_raw:
    if review['user_id'] in users_2016:
        business_ref = review['business_ref']
        ref_num = business_ref.split('_')[1]
        business_id = f'businessid_{ref_num}'
        
        categories = business_categories.get(business_id, ['Other'])
        for category in categories:
            category_counts[category] += 1

# Step 4: Get top 5
top_5 = sorted(category_counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:5]

# Format final answer
result = "Top 5 business categories by total reviews from 2016-registered users:\n\n"
for i, (category, count) in enumerate(top_5, 1):
    result += f"{i}. {category}: {count} reviews\n"

print(result)"}
